description: Output contract for StoryLab essay analysis. Defines required structure for LLM analysis output.

analysis.rubric_scores: array of exactly 8 objects, one per rubric_id (R001–R008). Each object must include:

evidence_spans.why_it_matters: string (brief explanation of why this evidence supports the score)

rubric_scores_items.notes: string (short internal note for tutor reference)

analysis.weakest_dimensions: array of 1-3 rubric IDs (strings) that the model identifies as the most limiting issues (must be from R001–R008)

dominant_misconception.misconception_id: string (must match one from misconceptions.json at runtime)

evidence_spans.why_it_matters: string (brief explanation of why this signals the misconception)

recommended_intervention.intervention_id: string (must match one from interventions.json at runtime)
recommended_intervention.rationale: string (internal, 1-3 sentences tied to misconception and weakest rubric dimensions)
recommended_intervention.output_format: string (what the student must produce, copied from interventions.json)

student_output.headline: string (one sentence, non-judgmental, e.g., 'Your essay has strong scenes but needs clearer connections between events.')
student_output.brief_explanation: string (2-4 sentences; may reference metaphor like Michelangelo/David if relevant, but keep short)

one_assignment.title: string (e.g., 'Force because-chain')
one_assignment.instructions:
string (bulleted list as single string with 
 for newlines, e.g., '• Step 1
• Step 2
• Step 3')
one_assignment.success_check: string (how student knows it worked, 1-2 sentences)

student_output.optional_next_step: string (one sentence, optional, can be empty string)

safety_flags: array of strings (e.g., ['possible_unresolved_crisis']), can be empty array
meta.model_limits: string (explicitly state: 'Do not rewrite the full essay; at most provide micro-snippets (≤2 sentences) only when necessary.')

evidence_spans.quote: Then I joined the debate team. Then I started volunteering.
evidence_spans.why_it_matters: Events are sequenced with 'then' but lack causal links showing why one led to the next.

rubric_scores.notes: Chronological structure without clear turning points or cause-and-effect relationships.

evidence_spans.quote: Then I joined the debate team. Then I started volunteering.
evidence_spans.why_it_matters: Some reflection present but stays at surface level without deeper psychological specificity.

rubric_scores.notes: First 'why' is present but second 'why' is missing.

evidence_spans.quote: Then I joined the debate team. Then I started volunteering.
evidence_spans.why_it_matters: Specific names and places mentioned, though could use more concrete objects.

rubric_scores.notes: Good use of named places and people.

evidence_spans.quote: Then I joined the debate team. Then I started volunteering.
evidence_spans.why_it_matters: Achievements mentioned but interpretation is minimal.

rubric_scores.notes: Some meaning-making present but could go deeper.

evidence_spans.quote: Then I joined the debate team. Then I started volunteering.
evidence_spans.why_it_matters: Some abstract claims present without sufficient scene-level proof.

evidence_spans.quote: Then I joined the debate team. Then I started volunteering.
evidence_spans.why_it_matters: Language is clear and readable, though some adverb use could be tightened.

rubric_scores.notes: Generally strong prose with minor verb/adverb issues.

evidence_spans.quote: Then I joined the debate team. Then I started volunteering.
evidence_spans.why_it_matters: Tone is consistent but could benefit from strategic variation.

evidence_spans.quote: Then I joined the debate team. Then I started volunteering.
evidence_spans.why_it_matters: Appropriate boundaries maintained, no concerning disclosures.

evidence_spans.quote: Then I joined the debate team. Then I started volunteering.
evidence_spans.why_it_matters: Transitions rely on 'then' rather than showing cause-and-effect, signaling A-and-B storytelling misconception.

dominant_misconception.why_this_matters: The essay reads as a list of events rather than a story with causal relationships. This prevents depth and coherence.

recommended_intervention.rationale: R001 is weakest (score 2), and M001 (A-and-B storytelling) is clearly present. I002 (Force because-chain) directly addresses the missing causal links.
recommended_intervention.output_format: Write 3 sentences connecting Event A → Event B using 'because' clauses. If you can't, restructure the events.

student_output.headline: Your essay has strong individual scenes but needs clearer connections showing why events led to each other.
student_output.what_to_fix_first: Add explicit 'because' links between your main events to show cause-and-effect, not just sequence.
student_output.brief_explanation: Right now your essay reads like a timeline: this happened, then that happened. Great stories show why A led to B. Think of it like carving a statue—you need to choose your 'David' (the core story) and remove everything that doesn't serve it. Start by connecting your events with 'because' clauses.

one_assignment.title: Force because-chain
one_assignment.instructions:
• Identify your 3 main events in the essay
• Write 3 sentences connecting Event A → Event B using 'because' clauses
• If you can't connect two events with 'because', restructure them
one_assignment.success_check: You'll know it worked when removing one event would break your story, and each event clearly causes the next.

student_output.optional_next_step: After connecting events, we can work on deepening the psychological 'why' behind your choices.

meta.privacy_note: Do not store essay text. Analysis metadata only.
meta.model_limits: Cite specific evidence from the essay. Do not rewrite the full essay; at most provide micro-snippets (≤2 sentences) only when necessary. Do not invent facts about the student or their experiences.
